macro_segments.title: Introduction to Forbidden Foods and the Transition from Avodah Zarah Concerns to Kashrus Issues

micro_segments.title: Overview of the new section focusing on forbidden foods and kashrus rather than avodah zarah interactions
The distinction between foods forbidden to eat vs. foods forbidden to derive benefit from (mutar b'hana'ah)
Introduction to wine of non-Jews as the primary forbidden item in this mishna

macro_segments.title: The Source and Dual Rationales for the Prohibition of Non-Jewish Wine

micro_segments.title: Gemara's derivation of the prohibition on wine of non-Jews from the verse about sacrifices and libations
Tosafot's question about why a regular mishna on stam yeinam cites the yayin nesech source
Tosafot's explanation that the primary gezeira is because of intermarriage (benot) concerns, with yayin nesech added as an additional layer
Discussion of how the two categories (yayin nesech and benot concerns) both matter halachically in different scenarios

macro_segments.title: Wine Vinegar and the Principle that Forbidden Status Persists

micro_segments.title: The case of wine vinegar that started as wine and the principle that forbidden status does not change when the object is transformed
Rav Ashi's clarification that a single seal is sufficient for our vinegar in non-Jewish hands, proving no concern of libations

macro_segments.title: Cooked Wine (Yayin Mevushal) and Mixtures: Object-Status vs. Handling Concerns

micro_segments.title: Rav Ila's teaching that cooked wine of non-Jews is forbidden if they had it while it was still wine
The principle that once something attains forbidden status, cooking or transformation does not remove that status
Rav Ashi's teaching that cooked wine in the hands of non-Jews from the start does not become forbidden
Explanation of the single seal rule for cooked wine: no concern of libations or tamper-swapping
Discussion of mixtures (alontit) added to wine and the same principle: forbidden if the non-Jew had the wine first
Definition of alontit as a medicinal mixture distinct from anumalin (spiced wine for drinking)

macro_segments.title: Exposure (Giluy) Concerns: Snakes and Poison in Wine and Water

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the gemara's shift from yayin nesech concerns to giluy (exposure) concerns due to danger from snakes
Rava and Rav Yosef's statements: mixed wine has no giluy problem; cooked wine has no yayin nesech problem
Rav Yaakov bar Idi's teaching that cooked wine is not a problem for exposure
Story of Rav Yannai bar Yishmael's illness and the question about cooked wine's exposure status
Shmuel and Avlat's story: Avlat's concern about not touching cooked wine due to yayin nesech prohibition
Rabbi Chiya's maidservant story confirming that cooked wine exposed is not a problem

macro_segments.title: Mixed Wine (Yayin Mazug): Snakes' Discernment and Varying Poison Behavior

micro_segments.title: Rav Ada bar Ahava's case of mixed wine exposed and Rav Papa's qualification about how well mixed
Story of Rabba bar Rav Huna seeing a snake cutting through water to drink wine and watering it down
Gemara's discussion of whether snakes will risk their lives for unmixed wine but not mixed wine
Story of a snake throwing water into a barrel to dilute the wine and access it
Rav Ashi's rejection of teretz for health/danger issues and Rava's final ruling on mixed wine

macro_segments.title: Exposure of Water and Foods: Cleanliness, Awareness, and Gender-Based Assumptions

micro_segments.title: Story of Rav Chelkiya bar Tuvi's attendant's exposed water and whether sleeping nearby prevents snake access
Rav's refusal to drink water from non-Jews' houses due to lack of giluy awareness
Rav would drink from a widow's house but not a non-Jew's, based on gender and habit assumptions
Shmuel's more stringent view refusing water from even widows' houses
Variant version: Rav distinguishes widow from non-Jew; Shmuel refuses both

macro_segments.title: Wines that are Not Susceptible to Giluy: Acrid, Bitter, Sweet, and Spoiled Wine

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi's three wines with no giluy concern: sharp (acrid), bitter, and sweet
Definitions and examples of the three non-giluy wines with various additives and sources
Rabbi Shimon ben Lakish and Rabbi Abahu on sweet wine (karina) from Asia
Rava's ruling that sweet wine from Asia has a giluy problem in its place of origin
Rava on wine that has begun to turn sour: first three days is a problem, after is not
Nehardean dissent: sour wine still has giluy problem even after three days

macro_segments.title: Unfermented Wine (Yayin Toses) and Grape Juice: Fermentation Status and Kashrus

micro_segments.title: Unfermented wine has no giluy problem but is a yayin nesech problem
Three-day window for unfermented wine before fermentation and changed status
Discussion of cress (shachalayim) as not a giluy problem but treated as forbidden in exile unless vinegar is added
Babylonian dip (kutach) as having no giluy problem but forbidden in exile
Rav Menashya on puncture marks in exposed foods as evidence of snake contact

macro_segments.title: Prevention of Exposure and Sound-Based Deterrents

micro_segments.title: Water or wine with constant dripping (tip-tip-tip) sound keeps snakes away
Figs with removed stems require no giluy concern based on Rabbi Eliezer's teaching

macro_segments.title: Snake Poison Types and Sinking Behavior: Young, Middle-Aged, and Elderly Snakes

micro_segments.title: Three types of snake poison: young snakes' poison sinks, middle-aged floats in middle, elderly floats at top
Tension: poison weakens with age but other animals grow stronger; resolution that poison specifically becomes weaker
Story of nine people drinking safely but the tenth dying: poison sinks to bottom
Similar case with melon and the danger of the tenth person eating from exposed food
Explanation of why three poison types matter: cannot assume safety based on number of safe consumers

macro_segments.title: Practical Restrictions on Handling Exposed Water and Detailed Conclusion

micro_segments.title: Prohibition on spilling exposed water in public domain, settling house dust, or making plaster with it
Restriction on giving exposed water to animals or washing hands, face, and feet
Dispute whether crevice-free body areas are permitted to wash or all areas are forbidden
Debate on whether one's own animal can drink exposed water versus a friend's animal
Resolution: cats can drink because they eat snakes; friend's cat status debated over weakness and resale value
